data.summary: GPU bilateral uses F16 input + 8-bit output: precision where it matters, compatibility where it doesn't
data.detail: After discovering Pixel 10 rejects F16 render targets, the hybrid approach was chosen: F16 input bitmap (full half-float precision going into shader), 8-bit ARGB_8888 output (compatible with all ImageReaders). This is acceptable because the bilateral output is a smoothed/blurred signal - the fine detail lives in the DIFFERENCE between full-precision original and the smoothed version. The 8-bit intermediate between horizontal/vertical passes is a known compromise.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Pair alignment exports use parallel format (left|right), not cross-eye -- settled after format confusion
data.detail: After confusion where cross-eye export plus preview half-swapping made images appear flat, the final decision was parallel format for pair alignment exports. Preview shows the image as-is with no swapping. This means the exported file has left eye on the left, right eye on the right.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: HuggingFace has pre-quantized FP16 model; fabio-sim has dynamic-shape model with different tensor names
data.detail: Two ONNX model sources were found: HuggingFace (static 518x518 FP16, tensor names pixel_values/predicted_depth) and fabio-sim (dynamic shape FP32, tensor names image/depth). The fabio-sim model can be fixed to arbitrary resolutions using onnx.make_input_shape_fixed(). Important: the API is make_input_shape_fixed, NOT make_dynamic_shape_fixed.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: XREAL SDK is Unity-only; glasses are just USB-C DisplayPort monitors requiring no SDK at all
data.detail: User asked to research XREAL SDK for better glasses integration. Research revealed the XREAL SDK targets Unity game engine exclusively and is completely irrelevant for native Android. The glasses function as standard USB-C DisplayPort monitors. Android's Presentation API is the correct approach for rendering to them as external displays. No special SDK, driver, or protocol needed.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User chose to keep Depth Anything V2 over V3, adding ViT-S (Small, 49MB) as fast default alongside ViT-B (Base, 188MB) quality option
data.detail: When asked about upgrading to Depth Anything V3, user decided to stick with V2 but add a Small model variant for faster results. ViT-S is 49MB FP16 (~4x smaller/faster) and is the default. ViT-B is 188MB FP16 for higher quality. Model switching is available in the advanced settings.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: MediaStore DATE_TAKEN doesn't work with photo picker URIs -- picker returns content://media/picker/ scheme
data.detail: Android's photo picker returns content://media/picker/ URIs that don't support MediaStore column queries for DATE_TAKEN. Initial implementation queried MediaStore columns from picker URIs and got null. Fixed by reading EXIF DateTimeOriginal from the image stream via ExifInterface instead. Additionally, MediaStore scanner overwrites DATE_TAKEN from the JPEG's EXIF data, so the fix also writes EXIF tags into the output JPEG before clearing IS_PENDING.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Android Canvas.drawBitmapMesh() is purpose-built for DIBR mesh warping — no manual triangle rasterization needed
data.detail: Research during Phase 2 planning revealed that Android's Canvas API has drawBitmapMesh(), which takes a mesh grid of displaced vertices and renders the warped bitmap directly. This eliminated the need to implement custom triangle rasterization for the depth-based displacement, significantly simplifying the SbsWarper implementation.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: ONNX Runtime createSession() has no progress callback — model init appears to hang with no way to show intermediate progress
data.detail: The model loading has two phases: file copy (has byte-level progress) and ORT session initialization (single blocking call, no progress API). With ALL_OPT the init took so long users thought the app was frozen. UX fix: show 'Copying model...' with progress bar during copy, then 'Optimizing model...' with indeterminate spinner during session init. Ultimately resolved by pre-optimizing offline so init is fast.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Photo Picker (PickVisualMedia API) needs no runtime permissions — scoped access handles it automatically
data.detail: During Phase 1 planning, discovered that Android's PickVisualMedia API provides scoped access to selected photos without requiring READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE or any other runtime permissions. Simplifies the image selection flow significantly.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Forcing 16:9 aspect ratio on StereoViewer did not fix glasses stretching - just zoomed in slightly
data.detail: First attempt to fix XREAL glasses stretching was to force the StereoViewer to render at 16:9 aspect ratio with letterboxing. User reported 'no difference, it just zooms in slightly, doesnt actually change the aspect ratio.' The approach was reverted and replaced with the Presentation API approach, which sends content directly to the glasses' external display at its native resolution.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: EXIF date must be written into JPEG before clearing IS_PENDING -- MediaStore scanner reads EXIF, not ContentValues
data.detail: Setting DATE_TAKEN in ContentValues alone doesn't persist because the MediaStore scanner overwrites it from the JPEG file's EXIF data. The correct approach: write DateTimeOriginal, DateTimeDigitized, DateTime, timezone offset, and subsecond tags into the JPEG via ExifInterface on a read-write FileDescriptor before clearing IS_PENDING, then re-assert DATE_TAKEN/DATE_ADDED/DATE_MODIFIED in the same update() call.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User rejected half-res bilateral filter — prefers full-res quality even at speed cost, forcing GPU shader migration
data.detail: Half-res bilateral (378x378) was implemented as a 4x speedup (~3s vs ~13s) but user explicitly asked to go back to full res. This forced the GPU shader (AGSL RuntimeShader) approach which achieved sub-1s at full resolution. Pattern: user consistently chooses quality over speed when both options exist.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: NNAPI is useless for Depth Anything V2 ViT model — missing LayerNorm and GELU op support
data.detail: NNAPI was planned for hardware acceleration of depth inference, but research showed it lacks support for LayerNorm and GELU operations used by Vision Transformer (ViT) models. NNAPI would just waste initialization time falling back to CPU anyway. Skipped entirely for Phase 1; CPU-only inference is the only viable path on Android for this model architecture.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: ComposeView in Presentation crashed 3 times before finding correct lifecycle owner setup
data.detail: Three failed attempts to get ComposeView working inside Android Presentation: (1) Setting lifecycle owner on window.decorView - didn't propagate to ComposeView. (2) Setting directly on ComposeView before setContent - still crashed. (3) User said 're-research this' after third crash. Final fix required creating a custom PresentationLifecycleOwner implementing all three interfaces (LifecycleOwner, SavedStateRegistryOwner, ViewModelStoreOwner) with explicit lifecycle management tied to Presentation callbacks. The missing piece was ViewModelStoreOwner - never set in earlier attempts.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Emulator CLI launch unreliable on Windows — headless mode gets stuck offline
data.detail: Attempting to launch the Android emulator from CLI on Windows resulted in it getting stuck in an offline state. Windowed mode was more reliable but still problematic. The pragmatic approach was to have the user launch the emulator from Android Studio Device Manager and then install via adb.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Full-res image loading caused 401MB SBS bitmap OOM crash -- Canvas can't render 8000+ pixel wide bitmaps
data.detail: Removing the 2048px cap to enable full-resolution export caused a crash. A 4032px source image produced an 8064px wide SBS bitmap (401MB). Compose Canvas choked rendering this. Initial fix (separate display vs full-res copies) still caused depth map diagonal artifacts because the 756x756 depth model distorts when processing stretched non-square full-res inputs.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Mesh warp replaced by per-pixel GPU shader warp (GpuStereoWarper) for higher quality — no mesh resolution limit
data.detail: Canvas.drawBitmapMesh was already GPU-accelerated but limited by mesh vertex resolution. User requested moving to a per-pixel AGSL shader that samples the depth map at every output pixel and shifts the source image accordingly. This eliminates the mesh resolution limit and gives pixel-perfect displacement. CPU fallback was retained in SbsWarper. Dead code from SbsResult mesh fields was cleaned up during the migration.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Phone USB-C port shared between ADB and XREAL glasses - cannot debug while glasses are connected
data.detail: The Pixel 10's single USB-C port is used both for ADB debugging and for the XREAL glasses connection. When glasses are plugged in for testing, the phone disconnects from ADB, preventing deployment and log inspection. This created a challenging develop-deploy-test cycle where builds had to be prepared before connecting glasses, and crash logs couldn't be read while reproducing glasses-specific issues.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Full SBS bitmap at 6144x4080 consumes ~96MB RAM — must explicitly recycle bitmaps
data.detail: Full SBS output doubles the width of the source image. For a typical phone photo (3072x4080), the result is 6144x4080 at 4 bytes/pixel = ~96MB per bitmap. Without explicit Bitmap.recycle() calls, regenerating SBS output a few times causes OOM. Android's GC doesn't reliably free native bitmap memory fast enough.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Surface detail default escalated from 0 to 0.5 to 1.0 across the session as user tested and approved the feature
data.detail: Surface detail started with no default (0), user requested 0.5 when it wasn't visually obvious enough, then after GPU acceleration made it fast, bumped to 1.0 as the default. Shows a pattern: user prefers aggressive defaults for enhancement features once they're working well, rather than conservative starting points.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Process at 2048px, export at full-res by reloading and scaling mesh vertices -- user's proposed architecture
data.detail: User proposed the final architecture after OOM and depth artifact failures: keep processing and preview at 2048px (proven safe), then at export time reload the source at target resolution, scale up the drawBitmapMesh vertex positions by the ratio, and apply the warp at full-res. Output scale selector (1x/0.5x/0.25x) controls the reload resolution. This separates preview quality from export quality cleanly.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: 756x756 depth model produces artifacts when source aspect ratio diverges significantly from square
data.detail: The depth model takes 756x756 input (square). Source images are naively stretched to fit. At 2048px cap this works fine, but at full resolution (e.g., 4032x3024, 4:3) the depth edges become distorted, producing visible diagonal line artifacts in the SBS output. This is why processing must stay at 2048px -- the aspect ratio distortion is more tolerable at that resolution.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User consistently chooses maximum quality over speed: full-res bilateral, 1022 depth model, FP32 depth, raw-depth detail extraction
data.detail: Across multiple decisions in this session, the user rejected speed optimizations that compromised quality: half-res bilateral, ViT-S as only model, normalized-depth surface detail. The user expects the system to find ways to be fast WITHOUT sacrificing quality (GPU shaders, pre-optimization), not to downgrade quality for speed. When a quality/speed tradeoff is presented, the user picks quality and expects engineering to solve the speed problem.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Convergence default changed from 1.0 to 0.5 per user request during glasses development
data.detail: User asked for convergence default to be 0.5 while working on glasses support. This was a quick config change in ProcessingConfig. The lower default provides a more subtle 3D effect that works better as a starting point for both cross-eye viewing and glasses viewing.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User prefers "bare minimum first, then iterate" — interrupted full scaffolding to request minimal launchable app
data.detail: When the assistant started creating the full project structure per the PRD, the user interrupted with "first just set up the bare minimum to have an app that launches on an emulator." This preference for incremental delivery over big-bang setup repeated throughout the session — Phase 1 before Phase 2, test on emulator before phone, etc.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Depth Anything V2 neural network cannot be an AGSL shader — it's a 98M parameter ViT requiring full forward pass, not a per-pixel op
data.detail: User asked if Depth Anything could be a GPU shader like the bilateral filter and warp. The answer is no: it's a Vision Transformer with ~98M parameters requiring a full neural network forward pass, fundamentally different from per-pixel shader operations. NNAPI delegate through ONNX Runtime is the only GPU acceleration path, but it proved incompatible (XNNPACK failed). The model inference remains CPU-bound.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Both pair alignment and auto 3D batch now export cross-eye format; parallel was removed as export option
data.detail: User noticed pair alignment exported in PARALLEL arrangement while auto 3D used CROSS_EYED. User wanted both to use cross-eye for consistency in batching. Single line change from Arrangement.PARALLEL to using default ProcessingConfig which is CROSS_EYED.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User wants discussion before planning — "first we talk through it, then once you have clarified my intent, we will make a plan"
data.detail: After the initial app launched, user explicitly stated: "lets get into the plan in the PRD. First we are going to talk through it, then once you have clarified my intent, we will make a technical implementation plan." This establishes a workflow: clarify intent via Q&A, then plan, then implement. Not jump straight to code.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Exported images must preserve source photo's date/time so they appear next to originals in album
data.detail: User requirement: all exported SBS images (both single and batch, both auto-3D and pair modes) must have their DATE_TAKEN match the input image's date. This ensures converted photos sort next to their originals in the phone's photo gallery rather than clustering at the export date.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: graphicsLayer scaling is post-rasterization GPU compositing with basic bilinear - no mipmaps, FilterQuality.High has no effect
data.detail: When investigating pixelation on glasses from the 0.5x horizontal squeeze, research revealed that graphicsLayer scaling is post-rasterization GPU compositing with only basic bilinear filtering. No mipmap support, and FilterQuality.High on BitmapPainter doesn't affect the graphicsLayer transform. The solution was to pre-build the half-SBS frame on CPU at exact target resolution (1920x1080), then display at 1:1 with zero GPU scaling.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: SBS warp uses symmetric displacement — both eyes shifted equally in opposite directions
data.detail: User asked whether both sides of SBS are shifted or only the right. Confirmed both eyes get shifted: left eye direction=-1 (shifts left), right eye direction=+1 (shifts right). This is standard DIBR creating symmetric parallax. User was satisfied with this approach. Later sessions may revisit to shift only right eye if needed.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Batch result thumbnails looked rough because loadThumbnail uses nearest-neighbor inSampleSize with no bilinear filtering step
data.detail: SBS images are 2x wide, so 1024px maxSize gives each eye only ~512px which then gets stretched to fill the preview card. The fix was switching from loadThumbnail to loadBitmapFromUri which uses createScaledBitmap(..., true) for bilinear filtering at up to 2048px. This is a recurring pattern: Android's default bitmap decoding (inSampleSize) uses nearest-neighbor and needs an explicit bilinear step for quality downscaling.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Default 3D strength changed from 5% to 2% -- subtler depth effect preferred as starting point
data.detail: The default 3D strength was reduced from 5% to 2% during this session, indicating the user prefers a more subtle starting point for the depth effect that can be manually increased rather than an aggressive default that needs dialing back.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Default parameters tuned through testing: strength 1%, smoothing 1 (no blur)
data.detail: Defaults evolved through user testing: started at 3% strength and 7 smoothing, user changed to 2% and 3, then 2% and 1, and finally 1% strength and 1 smoothing. User progressively wanted less effect and less blur, preferring subtle 3D with sharp depth boundaries. Smoothing of 1 means the blur kernel is skipped entirely.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User tests with deliberately challenging inputs to stress-test features before accepting them
data.detail: When testing stereo pair alignment, the user deliberately chose an askew photo to stress-test the feature. Similarly, full-res export was tested with high-res phone photos. The user validates features with edge cases, not just happy paths. Implementations should be robust to extreme inputs from the start.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: 3D viewer designed as reusable StereoViewer composable shared between HomeScreen preview tab and dedicated ViewerScreen
data.detail: User explicitly requested the stereo viewer be reusable: same component for the 3D Result tab (where users zoom in while adjusting parameters) and the dedicated full-screen viewer (with gallery, swipe navigation, AR glasses output). The dedicated viewer defaults to the SBS Converter output folder and supports swiping between images without returning to a gallery picker. Zoom always from center, not pinch centroid.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User provides architectural solutions when implementations fail -- proposed two-stage alignment and mesh-scaling export
data.detail: When the crop-after-alignment broke registration, the user proposed the two-stage approach (align, crop, translation-only refine). When full-res export OOM'd, the user proposed the process-at-2048/export-at-full-res-by-scaling-mesh-vertices architecture. The user thinks architecturally and their suggestions should be taken seriously as they often identify the correct solution faster than iterative debugging.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Switched from HuggingFace 518x518 FP16 to fabio-sim 518x518 FP32 for better ONNX graph compatibility
data.detail: After the 756x756 OOM and FP16 quantization failures, settled on using the fabio-sim dynamic model fixed to 518x518 in FP32. This is 371MB (vs 195MB FP16) but has a cleaner ONNX graph that supports BASIC_OPT optimization. Different tensor names (image/depth vs pixel_values/predicted_depth). APK size increase accepted for personal-use tool.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Pre-build 1920x1080 half-SBS frame on CPU for glasses instead of GPU scaling in StereoViewer
data.detail: To eliminate pixelation artifacts from graphicsLayer's 0.5x horizontal squeeze, the glasses pipeline was changed to pre-build the complete half-SBS frame on CPU. Each eye half is scaled to fit within 960x1080 maintaining aspect ratio using createScaledBitmap(filter=true) for bilinear filtering, composited side-by-side into a 1920x1080 bitmap, then displayed at 1:1 in the GlassesPresentation with halfSbsMode=false. This eliminates all GPU scaling artifacts.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Zoom from center of view, not pinch centroid — user found centroid-based zoom disorienting in stereo viewer
data.detail: The initial StereoViewer implementation zoomed toward the pinch centroid (standard behavior). User requested center-only zoom, so the centroid offset is now ignored and only scale + post-zoom pan are applied. This makes the zoom behavior predictable in the stereo split view where each pane needs to stay aligned.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Accepted naive 518x518 stretch for depth preprocessing — aspect-aware resize deferred
data.detail: Plan review flagged that stretching non-square images to 518x518 distorts aspect ratio and degrades depth quality. Options: accept distortion or implement resize-with-padding. User asked for explanation, then decided to accept naive stretch for Phase 1 and revisit if quality suffers. Documented in memory for future review.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User wants surface detail applied BEFORE edge smoothing blur — enhancement amplifies micro-detail, then blur smooths as needed
data.detail: User explicitly asked to remove the blur step before the surface detail pass and run surface detail at full resolution. The resulting pipeline order is: normalize -> equalize -> gamma -> bilateral unsharp mask (surface detail) -> blur (edge smoothing) -> warp. This preserves maximum detail from the enhancement step while still allowing post-enhancement smoothing via the separate Edge Smoothing control.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Delta-based transform sync for glasses avoids race conditions from reset-to-1x approach
data.detail: Code reviewer caught a fatal race condition in the initial zoom sync plan where phone would reset to 1x after rebuilding the glasses frame. The fix uses delta-based transforms: glasses always show phoneTransform/baseTransform ratio. Phone pane offsets are scaled to glasses pane dimensions to handle coordinate space differences. Debounced viewport-aware rebuild (200ms) crops visible region from high-res source for full detail at any zoom level.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: StereoAligner runs feature detection at 768px internally regardless of source resolution
data.detail: The stereo pair alignment algorithm internally downscales images to 768px for feature detection and matching. This means alignment quality is independent of source resolution, and full-res pair images can be loaded for export without affecting alignment accuracy. The alignment transform (rotation, scale, translation) is then applied to the full-res images.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Batch pair alignment needs no depth computation -- alignment uses feature matching, not depth
data.detail: When designing the pair alignment mode, the user noted depth computation probably wasn't needed for aligning real stereo pairs. This was confirmed: BoofCV feature matching (SURF descriptors + RANSAC) handles alignment without the depth model. However, the user asked to keep depth capability in consideration for future alignment improvements.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: ONNX transformer optimizer fusions help more on server GPUs than mobile CPUs — pre-optimized model was no faster on Pixel 10
data.detail: The offline transformer optimizer (attention, GELU, LayerNorm fusions) grew the model from 371MB to 376MB but provided no measurable inference speed improvement on Pixel 10. The fusions are designed for GPU execution providers (CUDA/TensorRT) where fused kernels reduce memory bandwidth. On CPU, the dominant cost is the raw compute volume of 98M parameters, not kernel launch overhead. FP16 conversion (376MB -> 188MB) was more impactful for memory/load time.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Plan review catches critical implementation bugs before coding — tensor shapes, lifecycle, displacement formula
data.detail: Subagent plan review identified 3 critical issues before implementation began: (1) output tensor is 3D Array<Array<FloatArray>> not 2D, (2) OrtEnvironment must stay alive alongside session (not wrapped in use{}), (3) displacement formula must be resolution-aware not fixed pixels. This validated the review-before-implement workflow required by CLAUDE.md.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Depth enhancement spec delivered via Discord attachment during async development session
data.detail: The user sent a 22KB markdown spec (depth_enhancement_implementation.md) via Discord attachment while the assistant was waiting for phone access. This shows the async development pattern: user drafts specs offline while assistant handles implementation. The spec introduced new depth processing parameters for the advanced tab with good defaults.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Gentle Gaussian blur on glasses view reduces pixelation: 0.5px at 1x zoom, fading to 0px at 2x zoom
data.detail: Even with pre-built frames, some pixelation/aliasing artifacts remained visible through glasses from the half-SBS squeeze. A dynamic Gaussian blur was added to the glasses StereoViewer via graphicsLayer renderEffect. Started at 4px, user iterated down to 1px, then 0.5px. Blur fades linearly to 0px at 2x zoom so zoomed-in detail is fully sharp. The blur is glasses-only (applyBlur parameter defaults false on phone viewer). renderEffect set to null when blur is 0 to avoid unnecessary GPU work.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Depth map caching in ViewModel enables fast SBS regeneration (~0.6s) after initial inference (~2.3s)
data.detail: Depth estimation is the expensive step (2.3s on emulator). Once cached in ViewModel state, SBS warp generation takes only 0.6s. This means users can adjust 3D strength and smoothing sliders and regenerate quickly without re-running inference. The plan review explicitly recommended this depth caching refactor in HomeViewModel.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: SBS preview in-app needed aspect ratio fix — FillWidth with horizontalScroll made portrait images enormous vertically
data.detail: User reported the SBS output looked "stretched weirdly" both in saved gallery image and in-app preview. The in-app issue was ContentScale.FillWidth inside a horizontalScroll container, making tall portrait SBS images render at full width and enormous height. Fixed by showing the full SBS image fitted to screen width with correct aspect ratio, no scrolling.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User iterates aggressively on visual quality: provides specific feedback and rapidly tests changes on real hardware
data.detail: Throughout the glasses development, the user tested every change on actual XREAL One Pro hardware and provided precise feedback: 'it just zooms in slightly', 'still crashing', 'reduce it to 1px blur', 'go down to 0.5px'. This pattern of rapid iteration with real-device testing drove the blur from 4px -> 1px -> 0.5px in three cycles. Development must account for this fast feedback loop and avoid over-engineering initial implementations.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Gradle wrapper JAR generation on Windows required using `gradle wrapper` command from installed distribution
data.detail: Setting up the Android project required the gradle-wrapper.jar. Copying from the Gradle installation didn't work (wrong jar). Had to run the `gradle wrapper` command from the installed Gradle 8.14 distribution to generate proper wrapper scripts and jar. Minor setup friction specific to bootstrapping a native Android project without Android Studio's wizard.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User says 're-research this' when multiple failed attempts indicate stale or incorrect assumptions
data.detail: After three failed attempts to fix ComposeView in Presentation, user explicitly told the assistant to 're-research this and ensure all your current assumptions align with current android documentation.' This pattern indicates the user expects the assistant to go back to primary sources rather than iterating on broken assumptions. The user is patient through 1-2 failures but escalates when a pattern of failure emerges.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: PRD has 5 phases; session focused on Phase 1 (depth map) and Phase 2 (SBS warp) only
data.detail: User confirmed starting with Phase 1 when asked about scope. After Phase 1 succeeded, naturally progressed to Phase 2 (SBS warp). Phases 3-5 (video processing, batch/queue, export/sharing) remain future work. This established the phased delivery approach for the project.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Adaptive depthSigma considered but per-pixel version deferred; global stddev-based sigma shipped instead
data.detail: The bilateral filter's fixed depthSigma=0.04 was too aggressive for long-distance scenes. An adaptive version was implemented that scales sigma based on global depth stddev (0.01-0.08). User asked if it could be more gradual/per-region. The per-pixel adaptive sigma in the AGSL shader was discussed as a future improvement but not implemented - it would scale sigma based on local depth value at each pixel, giving near objects tighter sensitivity and far objects looser sensitivity.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Test images bundled as assets (~22MB total: altar, church, coast) for easy depth testing without gallery access
data.detail: Three 2D test images from the user's collection were bundled as app assets for quick testing. The 3D reference versions exist for visual comparison of output quality. Having built-in test images avoids needing gallery access during initial development and provides consistent regression test inputs.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Model migration path: HuggingFace static FP16 -> fabio-sim dynamic -> fixed 756 FP32 (OOM) -> fixed 518 FP32
data.detail: The depth model went through four iterations in one session: started with HuggingFace FP16 (worked but NO_OPT only), tried fabio-sim dynamic for higher res, fixed to 756x756 FP32 (worked but OOM on emulator), fell back to 518x518 FP32 from fabio-sim (stable, BASIC_OPT works). Each step was driven by quality ambitions hitting runtime constraints. Higher resolution remains a goal for real devices.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: User's physical device is Pixel phone (device ID 57291FDCQ008KC) — app deployed to it at end of session
data.detail: At the end of the session, the user connected their physical phone via USB. The app was deployed directly to it. This device has more RAM than the emulator, meaning the 756x756 model may work on it. Testing shifted from emulator to real device for final parameter tuning.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Session d2afb2de: Project bootstrap through end-to-end SBS 3D image generation
data.detail:
First development session for the side-by-side 3D converter app. Started with a Flutter→Android pivot after the PRD specified native Kotlin+Compose. Bootstrapped a bare-minimum Android app, then implemented Phase 1 (Depth Anything V2 depth estimation via ONNX Runtime) and Phase 2 (mesh-based DIBR warping using Canvas.drawBitmapMesh, save-to-gallery, settings UI).

Key technical journey: The depth model went through four iterations — HuggingFace FP16 (ALL_OPT crash, fixed with NO_OPT), fabio-sim dynamic model for higher resolution, 756x756 FP32 (worked but OOM on emulator), and finally 518x518 FP32 from fabio-sim with BASIC_OPT. FP16 quantization of the fabio-sim model was attempted twice but produced broken Cast nodes. NNAPI was ruled out entirely (missing ViT ops).

User feedback shaped defaults: strength went from 3% to 1%, smoothing from 7 to 1. Half SBS was removed in favor of Full SBS only. User prefers minimal defaults with manual control. The "discuss before planning" and "bare minimum first" workflow patterns were established.

Critical bugs caught: output tensor 3D cast error, OOM from bitmap leaks on SBS regeneration, SBS preview aspect ratio distortion from FillWidth+horizontalScroll. Plan review by subagent caught tensor shape and OrtEnvironment lifecycle issues before coding began.

Session ended with the app deployed to user's physical Pixel phone, producing end-to-end 3D SBS images from 2D photos: image selection → depth estimation (2.3s) → settings adjustment → SBS generation (0.6s) → save to gallery. Three bundled test images (altar, church, coast) for quick testing.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Session 0d8b2fa0: Multi-day Discord development session covering UI redesign through AR glasses support
data.detail:
This was the project's second major development session, conducted over multiple days via Discord (starting ~March 20, 2026). It transformed the SBS Converter from a basic functional prototype into a polished 3D photo viewer with AR glasses support. The session covered approximately 10 major feature areas across 7+ commits.

**UI Redesign (Phase 1)**: The app was restructured from a scrollable multi-screen layout to a single-screen design. Test image selection was removed (phone gallery only). A depth reveal slider was added for before/after comparison, and a mesh wireframe toggle for visualizing warp offsets. 3D parameter controls were pinned to the bottom. The TopAppBar was removed to reclaim vertical space. A key layout discovery: aspectRatio calls need matchHeightConstraintsFirst=true to prevent tall image overflow.

**Tab Navigation & Auto-Generation**: Scrollable layout replaced with TabRow (Depth Compare, SBS Result, Wigglegram). The manual "Generate 3D" button was removed - SBS generation now triggers automatically after depth estimation and on slider release via debounced onSliderFinished(). Advanced settings (gamma, smoothing, arrangement) collapsed behind expandable section. DepthAnalyzer was added for auto-tuning then removed - the user rejects auto-tuning in favor of manual controls with good defaults (this happened twice across sessions).

**Depth Model Upgrades**: The depth model went through multiple upgrades: 756->770->1022 resolution, and a reversal from FP16 back to FP32. The 1022x1022 resolution (14x73 ViT patches) was chosen as the closest valid ViT multiple to 1024. The FP32 model is 382MB, bundled in the APK since it's a personal-use tool. NNAPI and XNNPACK delegates were discovered to be dead ends for this model.

**GPU Shader Pipeline**: Two AGSL RuntimeShaders were developed - a bilateral filter for depth smoothing and a stereo warp shader. A critical constraint was discovered: the Pixel 10 rejects RGBA_F16 render targets in ImageReader (PixelFormat 22). The solution: F16 input bitmaps (full precision into shader) with 8-bit output (acceptable for smoothed depth). Adaptive depthSigma was added based on global depth stddev, with per-pixel adaptive sigma discussed but deferred.

**Depth Enhancement (Surface Detail)**: A bilateral unsharp mask was implemented: original depth minus GPU-smoothed depth gives surface detail, which is added back at user-controlled strength. The user explored doing surface detail before normalization for long-distance scenes but this was deferred. The 8-bit precision of the intermediate bilateral passes was discussed extensively - the key insight is that the smoothed version only needs to be "good enough" since fine detail lives in the DIFFERENCE against the full-precision original.

**3D Viewer & Gallery**: A full image viewer was built with gallery grid (2-column thumbnails from left SBS half at 512px), single image stereo viewer with zoom/pan/swipe gestures, multi-select deletion, and auto-hiding controls. A critical gesture handling lesson: multiple pointerInput blocks fight over gesture ownership; everything must go in a single awaitEachGesture block. A Compose layout lesson: Modifier.align() only works on direct Box children, not through wrapper composables like TooltipBox. The viewer had a resolution bug where loadBitmapFromUri capped the WHOLE SBS image at 2048px (giving only 1024px per eye) - fixed by adding maxDimension parameter.

**AR Glasses Support (Presentation API)**: XREAL SDK research revealed it's Unity-only; the glasses are standard USB-C DisplayPort monitors. Android's Presentation API was used to render to the external display. The biggest challenge: ComposeView in Presentation crashed 3 times before finding the correct lifecycle owner setup. The solution required a custom PresentationLifecycleOwner implementing LifecycleOwner + SavedStateRegistryOwner + ViewModelStoreOwner (the missing ViewModelStoreOwner was the key breakthrough). A 16:9 aspect ratio force on StereoViewer was tried first but failed ("just zooms in slightly").

**Half SBS & Aspect Ratio**: The initial Half SBS approach used FillBounds for landscape and FillHeight for portrait, which was fragile. This was replaced with a universal ContentScale.Fit + scaleX*0.5 approach that pre-squeezes horizontally for all aspect ratios - glasses unsqueeze restores correct proportions.

**Glasses Quality Pipeline**: graphicsLayer scaling was found to use basic bilinear with no mipmaps, and FilterQuality.High has no effect on it. The fix: pre-build 1920x1080 half-SBS frames on CPU with createScaledBitmap(filter=true), display at 1:1 on glasses. Zoom/pan sync uses delta-based transforms (phoneTransform/baseTransform) to avoid race conditions, with debounced viewport-aware rebuild at 200ms. A gentle Gaussian blur (0.5px at 1x, fading to 0px at 2x zoom) was added to smooth remaining artifacts, tuned through three user iterations (4px -> 1px -> 0.5px).

**Development Constraints**: The Pixel 10's single USB-C port is shared between ADB and XREAL glasses, making it impossible to debug while glasses are connected. This forced a pre-build-then-test workflow. The user showed a strong pattern of rapid real-device iteration with precise visual feedback, and escalates to "re-research this" when multiple failures indicate stale assumptions.

Key commits: 2db7ee4 (depth model + convergence), 67081aa (convergence viz), 21b3bd5 (DepthAnalyzer, later removed), 908bab3 (UI streamline), 5edc8ed (gallery + viewer + batch fixes), e59084a (1022 FP32 model), 6424b09 (GPU bilateral F16/adaptive sigma), 7a43ca9 (Presentation API glasses), 102ba18 (zoom/pan sync + bilinear).
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Compose BlurEffect on glasses Presentation view doesn't effectively reduce aliasing from 0.5x horizontal scaling
data.detail: Tried BlurEffect at 0.5-4px radius in graphicsLayer on the StereoPane when rendering to XREAL glasses via Presentation API. The Gaussian blur doesn't adequately address the aliasing artifacts caused by the 0.5x horizontal pre-squeeze in Half SBS mode. The blur either has no visible effect at low radius (0.5px) or makes the image noticeably soft without actually fixing the pixel-level aliasing. Need to explore alternative anti-aliasing approaches: AGSL shader-based downscaling with proper filtering, MSAA, or pre-filtering the source image before display.
data.author: claude

data.summary: Compose BlurEffect (0.5-4px) doesn't fix aliasing from Half SBS 0.5x horizontal scaling on XREAL glasses
data.detail: Gaussian blur via graphicsLayer renderEffect either too soft (visible blur at 4px) or imperceptible (0.5px). The aliasing is pixel-level from the scaleX=0.5 pre-squeeze — a post-render blur can't undo sampling artifacts already baked in. Need pre-filtering before the scale, or shader-based downsampling with proper AA.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Use down-up round-trip filtered bitmaps (same dimensions) instead of overlay for Half SBS anti-aliasing
data.detail: Scale each half-image W→W/2→W with bilinear filtering to remove frequencies that alias under scaleX=0.5f. Single layer replaces full-res painter when halfSbsMode is on. No overlay needed because at 2x zoom scaleX=1.0 (no downsampling, zero aliasing). Same dimensions as originals ensures ContentScale.Fit alignment is preserved.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Down-up bitmap round trip as a low-pass filter: scale down with bilinear to remove high frequencies, scale back up to original dimensions
data.detail: When GPU compositing downscales without mipmaps (e.g., graphicsLayer scaleX=0.5), pre-filter the source by scaling W→W/2→W with createScaledBitmap(filter=true). The round trip removes frequencies above Nyquist for W/2, preventing aliasing during subsequent GPU squeeze. Output has identical dimensions to source, preserving all layout/transform calculations.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: XREAL AR glasses support: Presentation API, Half SBS display, anti-aliasing, zoom/pan sync
data.detail:
Implemented full AR glasses support for the SBS 3D Converter app, targeting XREAL One Pro glasses connected via USB-C DisplayPort.

Research phase: Investigated XREAL SDK — found it's Unity-only, irrelevant for native Android. The glasses are a standard USB-C DP monitor (1920x1080 per eye). 3D mode requires 16:9 Half SBS input where each half is horizontally squeezed, then the glasses unsqueeze.

Presentation API: Created GlassesPresentation extending android.app.Presentation to render directly to the glasses display at native resolution, bypassing phone screen mirroring. Required a custom PresentationLifecycleOwner implementing LifecycleOwner + SavedStateRegistryOwner + ViewModelStoreOwner because ComposeView needs all three ViewTree owners. Initial attempts setting owners on the decorView or ComposeView directly failed — the fix was setting all three on window.decorView before setContentView. Lifecycle events are managed explicitly (ON_CREATE in onCreate, ON_RESUME in onStart, ON_DESTROY in dismiss).

Display detection: DisplayManager.getDisplays() detects non-default displays. DisplayListener handles connect/disconnect. A glasses toggle button (👓) appears in the viewer toolbar when an external display is detected.

Half SBS aspect ratio: ContentScale.Fit + scaleX=0.5f in graphicsLayer correctly pre-squeezes for the glasses' unsqueeze. Tried ContentScale.FillBounds — broke aspect ratio for non-16:9 images. The Fit+scaleX approach preserves correct proportions for all aspect ratios.

Anti-aliasing journey: The scaleX=0.5f GPU compositing uses bilinear-without-mipmaps, causing visible aliasing on the glasses. Attempted fixes that failed: (1) Compose BlurEffect at 0.5-4px — post-render blur can't undo sampling artifacts already baked in. (2) Two-layer overlay with pre-scaled W/4 bitmaps — ContentScale.Fit calculated different positions for different source dimensions, layers couldn't align. (3) FillBounds on both layers — fixed alignment but broke aspect ratio.

Final solution: Down-up bitmap round trip (W→W/2→W with bilinear filtering). This removes frequencies above the Nyquist limit for W/2, preventing aliasing during the subsequent 0.5x GPU squeeze. Output has identical dimensions to the original, so ContentScale.Fit layout is preserved. Single filtered layer replaces the full-res painter when halfSbsMode is on — no overlay needed because at 2x zoom scaleX=1.0 (zero aliasing, filtered and original are identical).

Zoom/pan sync: Phone's StereoViewer reports transform changes via onTransformChanged callback. GlassesPresentation receives external scale/offsetX/offsetY state. The glasses' StereoViewer uses external state (gestures disabled) and follows phone transforms. Swipe navigation and image changes also sync.

Additional viewer controls: Half SBS toggle (H button), eye swap toggle (⇄), delete with confirmation (🗑️), glasses toggle (👓) — all in a centered bottom toolbar row. Controls auto-hide after 4 seconds, tap to show/hide.

Key constraint: Pixel 10's single USB-C port is shared between ADB and XREAL glasses, making it impossible to debug while glasses are connected. All glasses testing required disconnect/reconnect cycles.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Add "delete right images after processing" feature + unified MediaDeleteHelper
data.detail:
Added option to delete right-side source images after batch pair alignment processing. Created MediaDeleteHelper.kt utility that centralizes all image deletion logic across the app:

1. deleteOwnedImages() — for app-created files in Pictures/SBS Converter (used by ViewerScreen). Catches SecurityException specifically instead of swallowing all exceptions.
2. resolvePickerUri() — converts Photo Picker URIs (content://media/picker/...) to real MediaStore URIs. Two-step strategy: first queries the picker URI for _ID column (picker grants read access), then falls back to extracting numeric ID from lastPathSegment. No permissions needed.
3. createDeleteRequest() — wraps MediaStore.createDeleteRequest() for API 30+ system confirmation dialog. Handles batch deletion with a single dialog.

Refactored ViewerScreen.deleteImages() to use shared utility instead of bare contentResolver.delete() with silent exception swallowing.

BatchViewModel gets: _deleteRightImages toggle (default false), Channel-based one-shot event for delete URIs (avoids double-dialog on config change), snapshotted pending pair IDs to prevent re-deleting from previous processing runs. URI resolution and channel send happen before _isProcessing goes false.

BatchScreen gets: Material3 Switch toggle visible only in PAIR_ALIGN mode on API 30+, StartIntentSenderForResult launcher for system delete dialog, LaunchedEffect collecting from Channel, unresolved count feedback text.

Key decisions: no new permissions needed, toggle gated on API 30+, defaults to OFF (manual control preference), best-effort deletion with graceful degradation.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: createDeleteRequest grants permission but file persists in gallery on API 36 (Pixel 10 Pro XL)
data.detail: Attempted to delete right-side source images after batch pair alignment using MediaStore.createDeleteRequest(). URI resolution worked perfectly (picker URI → _ID query → MediaStore URI). System dialog appeared and user confirmed. Logs showed "Granted permission to 1 items" and MediaStore entry query returned "No result found" — suggesting the MediaStore row was removed. However the user reports the image still shows in their gallery. Tried two approaches: (1) trusting createDeleteRequest to handle deletion automatically (per official docs), and (2) calling contentResolver.delete() after permission grant (throws SecurityException: "has no access"). Neither resulted in the file actually disappearing from the user's perspective on Android 16 (API 36). Possible causes: gallery caching, physical file not removed despite MediaStore row deletion, or behavioral change in API 36. Reverted all changes.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Photo Picker URI _ID query works: picker grants read access, returns MediaStore row ID
data.detail: ContentResolver.query(pickerUri, arrayOf(MediaStore.MediaColumns._ID)) successfully returns the MediaStore row ID from a Photo Picker URI on API 36. Picker URI format: content://media/picker/0/com.android.providers.media.photopicker/media/{picker_id}. The _ID returned differs from the picker path segment (e.g., picker path=10449 but _ID=314025). This is the reliable resolution strategy — lastPathSegment parsing gives the wrong ID.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Precision-preserving depth pipeline refactor: F16 encoding, linear normalization, scene-aware disparity
data.detail:
Refactored the depth processing pipeline to preserve linear depth ratios and eliminate guesswork in stereo offset tuning.

**Problem:** The pipeline normalized raw depth to [0,1], then applied histogram equalization (CDF redistribution) and gamma remapping. These non-linear transforms destroyed depth ratios — a pixel 10x farther got compressed to ~1.5x in equalized space. This forced per-image slider tweaking and spawned a cascade of workarounds: bilateral unsharp mask to recover lost micro-detail, gamma slider to compensate for equalization distortion.

**Key discovery:** Depth Anything v2 outputs inverse depth (disparity-like values) where closer objects have higher values. The raw ratios are physically meaningful and directly proportional to stereo parallax. Normalization + equalization was destroying this.

**Changes made:**

1. **ImageProcessor pipeline simplified:** normalize→blur→warp (removed equalizeDepthHistogram, remapDepthGamma, bilateralUnsharpMask calls). Raw depth range computed and passed through to warper.

2. **GpuStereoWarper upgraded to F16 depth input:** Replaced 8-bit grayscale encoding (256 depth levels) with RGBA_F16 bitmap encoding (~2048 levels). Adopted proven pattern from GpuBilateralFilter.floatArrayToBitmap(). F16 input bitmaps work on Pixel 10; only F16 render targets are rejected.

3. **Scene-aware disparity formula:** `maxDisparity = depthScale / 100 * width / 2 * rawRange`. The rawRange multiplier means deeper scenes automatically get more parallax — a landscape with rawRange=5 gets ~3x more parallax than a close-up with rawRange=1.5 at the same slider setting.

4. **Warp shader simplified:** Removed gradient micro-parallax uniforms (hasGradient, gradientScale, gradientMap). Natural surface detail is preserved since we no longer flatten it with equalization.

5. **ProcessingConfig reduced:** Removed depthGamma and surfaceDetail fields. UI sliders reduced from 5 to 3 (3D Strength, Convergence, Edge Smoothing + Arrangement toggle).

6. **Code preserved but not called:** DepthEnhancer (bilateral USM, gradient micro-parallax), GpuBilateralFilter, BitmapUtils.equalizeDepthHistogram/remapDepthGamma all kept in codebase per user request.

**Risk:** depthScale slider range (0.5-8.0) may need recalibration since rawRange multiplier amplifies effective disparity. Needs empirical tuning on XREAL glasses.

**Files modified:** ProcessingConfig.kt, ImageProcessor.kt, SbsWarper.kt, GpuStereoWarper.kt, HomeViewModel.kt, HomeScreen.kt

data.summary: Use Android Foreground Service (not WorkManager) for backgroundable batch processing
data.detail: Foreground Service is simpler and more direct for user-initiated, time-bounded processing. WorkManager adds retry/reschedule complexity we don't need. The mediaProcessing type has a 6-hour limit on Android 15+ which is more than enough. START_NOT_STICKY since in-memory state is lost on process death anyway.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: BatchProcessingState as single source of truth for batch state, owned by SbsApplication
data.detail: Rejected dual ownership (ViewModel + shared state) because Activity recreation via notification tap would cause state divergence. Single ownership in SbsApplication follows the existing pattern (depthEstimator, imageProcessor). ViewModel becomes a thin coordinator reading shared state flows.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: GPU shaders (HardwareRenderer+ImageReader) and ONNX Runtime work in Foreground Service without Activity
data.detail: HardwareRenderer renders to any Surface including ImageReader's surface — it's headless GPU rendering with no Activity dependency. ONNX Runtime is pure CPU. Both continue working when the app is backgrounded as long as the process is alive via the foreground service.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: BatchProcessingService must NOT use android:process — same-process required for URI grants and ONNX session
data.detail: Photo Picker URI grants are inherited by the service only if it runs in the same process. Separate process would also require IPC to share the ONNX Runtime session. Never add android:process to the service declaration.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Companion isServiceRunning flag for staleness detection on ViewModel recreation
data.detail: Service sets companion var isServiceRunning in onCreate/onDestroy. ViewModel init checks if shared state says isProcessing but service isn't running (crash recovery). Reverts PROCESSING items to PENDING and resets state. Simpler than ActivityManager.getRunningServices().
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Backgroundable batch processing via Android Foreground Service
data.detail:
Batch processing previously ran in viewModelScope.launch inside BatchViewModel. When the user switched apps, Android could kill the process, losing all progress — a real problem when processing dozens of images.

Researched background processing options: Foreground Service vs WorkManager vs regular background service. Chose Foreground Service with mediaProcessing type (Android 15+, 6-hour limit) as the simplest, most direct approach for user-initiated time-bounded work.

Key research finding: GPU shaders (HardwareRenderer + ImageReader) are headless rendering with no Activity dependency, and ONNX Runtime is pure CPU — both work fine in a Foreground Service. No changes to the processing pipeline itself were needed.

Implementation (7 files, +547/-264 lines):

1. Created BatchProcessingState.kt — app-scoped single source of truth for all batch state (items, progress, status). Uses MutableStateFlow fields and AtomicLong for ID generation. Reviewer caught that dual ownership (ViewModel + shared state) would break on Activity recreation — fixed by making shared state the sole owner.

2. Created BatchProcessingService.kt — Foreground Service that owns the processing coroutine. Moved both processing loops (AUTO_3D and PAIR_ALIGN) from BatchViewModel. Shows persistent notification with progress bar, cancel action, and content intent to reopen app. Completion notification when done. Implements onTimeout() for Android 15+ 6-hour limit. Uses companion isServiceRunning flag for staleness detection.

3. Updated AndroidManifest.xml — added FOREGROUND_SERVICE, FOREGROUND_SERVICE_MEDIA_PROCESSING, POST_NOTIFICATIONS permissions and service declaration with foregroundServiceType="mediaProcessing".

4. Refactored BatchViewModel.kt — stripped from ~340 to ~120 lines. Now a thin coordinator that reads shared state flows and sends start/cancel intents to the service. Includes staleness detection in init block for crash recovery.

5. Updated BatchScreen.kt — added POST_NOTIFICATIONS permission request (Android 13+) before starting. Removed BackHandler that blocked navigation during processing since the service handles it now. Back button always visible.

6. Updated SbsApplication.kt — added batchProcessingState property and notification channel creation (IMPORTANCE_LOW, no sound).

7. Added concurrent processing guard to HomeViewModel.kt — blocks depth inference (onImageSelected, generateSbs) while batch service is running to prevent ONNX Runtime thread safety issues. Exposes isBatchProcessing StateFlow for UI.

Critical constraint documented: service must never use android:process attribute — same-process required for Photo Picker URI grant inheritance and shared ONNX session.

Built and installed on Pixel 10 Pro XL, user confirmed it works perfectly.

data.summary: MediaStore queries without READ_MEDIA_IMAGES only return app-created entries, hiding externally-placed or orphaned images
data.detail: Under Android scoped storage, apps can query MediaStore for their own entries without permissions. But images placed by file manager, ADB, other apps, or orphaned after app reinstall are invisible without READ_MEDIA_IMAGES (API 33+) or READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE (API 26-32).
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Auto-request media permission on gallery open via LaunchedEffect, matching BatchScreen notification permission pattern
data.detail: Permission is requested automatically when GalleryView opens. This is standard UX for a gallery screen. Denied state shows a "Grant Access" button fallback. Pattern mirrors BatchScreen.kt's rememberLauncherForActivityResult for POST_NOTIFICATIONS.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Fix Viewer gallery to show ALL images from SBS Converter folder
data.detail:
User reported the Viewer gallery was not showing all photos in the Pictures/SBS Converter folder. Root cause: the app had no READ_MEDIA_IMAGES or READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE permissions declared. Under Android's scoped storage model, MediaStore queries without these permissions only return entries the app itself created in the current installation. Images placed by file managers, ADB, other apps, or orphaned after app reinstall were invisible.

Fix applied to two files:
1. AndroidManifest.xml — Added READ_MEDIA_IMAGES (API 33+) and READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE with maxSdkVersion=32 (API 26-32). No WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE needed since saves use contentResolver.insert(). No READ_MEDIA_VISUAL_USER_SELECTED needed since we're not browsing the user's whole photo library.
2. ViewerScreen.kt — Added three pieces: (a) ViewModel gains hasMediaPermission StateFlow, checkMediaPermission(), and onPermissionResult() methods. (b) GalleryView composable auto-requests permission on open via LaunchedEffect + rememberLauncherForActivityResult, following the same pattern as BatchScreen.kt's notification permission. (c) Empty state updated to show "Grant photo access to see all images" with a retry button when permission is denied.

Key insight from review: READ_MEDIA_IMAGES grants access to ALL images on device, which is broader than needed, but there's no folder-scoped alternative in Android's permission model. The RELATIVE_PATH LIKE query still scopes results to the SBS Converter folder.

data.summary: Use createWriteRequest + manual delete instead of createDeleteRequest for non-owned image deletion
data.detail: createDeleteRequest on API 36 removed MediaStore rows but left physical files (previous attempt reverted). createWriteRequest grants write access, then contentResolver.delete() uses the same code path that works in ViewerScreen for app-owned images. Two-phase: try direct delete first, fall back to createWriteRequest on SecurityException.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Implement delete right-side images after batch pair alignment
data.detail: Added feature to optionally delete right-side source images after batch pair alignment processing. Created new MediaDeleteHelper utility (util/MediaDeleteHelper.kt) with Photo Picker URI resolution (picker URI → MediaStore URI via _ID query) and two-phase deletion (direct contentResolver.delete first, createWriteRequest fallback on SecurityException). Added deleteRightImages toggle to BatchProcessingState. Added deletion orchestration to BatchViewModel (attemptDeleteRightImages, pendingDeleteUris flow, write permission callbacks). Added Material3 Switch toggle to BatchScreen visible only in Pair Align mode on API 30+. Processing completion detected via LaunchedEffect on isProcessing with wasProcessing guard. System dialog for write permission launched via ActivityResultLauncher. Transient status text auto-clears after 5s. Files: MediaDeleteHelper.kt (new), BatchProcessingState.kt, BatchViewModel.kt, BatchScreen.kt.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: createWriteRequest + contentResolver.delete also fails on API 36 — file persists despite granted permission
data.detail: Second attempt at deleting non-owned images. createWriteRequest system dialog appeared, user confirmed, but physical file persisted in gallery — identical behavior to previous createDeleteRequest attempt. Also researched RELATIVE_PATH update for move: unreliable for non-owned files, can make files disappear or silently no-op. All scoped storage write operations on non-owned files appear broken or unreliable on API 36. Feature is blocked by platform limitation.
